notes.note: This is the younger L. Cornelius Balbus, quaestor of Further Spain under Pollio, nephew of the elder Balbus, Caesar's intimate. He absconded with the provincial treasury into Mauretania.
Bogudes (Bogud) was king of western Mauretania in North Africa, a Caesarian client; Balbus crossed the strait at the Pillars of Hercules to take refuge in his realm.

notes.anchor: the same things Quintus Caesar did
notes.note: "Quintus Caesar" is a manuscript corruption for Q. Cassius Longinus, the rapacious Caesarian governor of Further Spain in 48-47 BC whose extortions provoked a mutiny; Pollio holds him up as the byword for misrule that Balbus is now imitating.

notes.anchor: led him down to a seat in the fourteen rows
notes.note: By the lex Roscia theatralis (67 BC) the first fourteen rows of seats in the theatre were reserved for the equestrian order; seating an actor there (and giving him the gold ring of a knight) usurped a privilege only the state could confer.

notes.anchor: he extended his own four-year magistracy for himself
notes.note: The quattuorvirate was the board of four chief magistrates of a Roman municipality; Balbus, holding such an office in a Spanish town, illegally prolonged his own tenure and rigged the local elections.

An earlier proconsul of Further Spain (probably in the Sullan civil wars) under whom a local Senate was massacred or expelled; Balbus restored from exile the partisans of that bygone upheaval rather than of the present one.

notes.anchor: a \textit{praetexta} drawn from his own journey
notes.note: A fabula praetexta was a Roman historical drama on a native (rather than Greek) subject; Balbus's vanity in staging a play about his own diplomatic mission, and weeping at it, struck Pollio as beyond even Caesar's example.

notes.anchor: I am born a Roman citizen
notes.note: The cry asserts the citizen's legal immunity from summary execution and from being thrown to the beasts; Balbus's mocking reply ("go appeal to the people's good faith") flouts the most basic protection of Roman law, the right of provocatio.
